frameworks.name: continuous attractor networks
frameworks.one_line: one-dimensional bump-attractor model storing continuous-valued working memory

frameworks.name: short-term synaptic plasticity
frameworks.one_line: short-term facilitation and depression of recurrent synapses modulate bump stability

frameworks.name: diffusion-drift decomposition
frameworks.one_line: slow bump dynamics decomposed into spike-noise diffusion plus heterogeneity-driven drift

frameworks.name: mutual information
frameworks.one_line: used to quantify retained working-memory content

core_question: How do short-term facilitation and depression of recurrent synapses jointly shape noise-induced degradation of continuous working memory in attractor networks?
key_assumptions: memory states live on a continuous one-dimensional manifold
network operates close to a stationary state where slow dynamics are tractable
noise arises from spiking variability plus quenched connectivity/neuronal heterogeneity
theory applies to both rate models and spiking networks
manipulations: presence and strength of short-term facilitation
presence and strength of short-term depression
level of spiking variability and connectivity heterogeneity
key_findings: slow bump dynamics decompose into spike-driven diffusion and heterogeneity-driven directed drift
short-term facilitation reduces both diffusion and directed drift
short-term depression tends to increase both diffusion and drift
theory predicts attractor sensitivity to distractor inputs and conditions for stable retention
interpretations: the combined balance of facilitation and depression controls how reliably continuous attractors store working memory
mutual information across the facilitation/depression regime indexes the network's working-memory capacity
